Comment: Tiltrotor VTOL -- four tilting rotors, four control surfaces (split ailerons, elevator, rudder) and four tilt servos -- on a RealFlight custom model. The "px4" values are HIL_ACTUATOR_CONTROLS.controls[] indices and MUST match PWM_MAIN_FUNC* in ROMFS/px4fmu_common/init.d-posix/airframes/1215_flightaxis_tiltrotor (and HIL_ACT_FUNC* in the .hil twin). DESIGN RULE: this table is a PURE IDENTITY PIPE for all twelve channels - rf N == px4 N, RealFlight channel N+1 is driven by PX4 output channel N+1, with no renumbering and no reversal anywhere. WHAT each channel carries is decided ENTIRELY by PWM_MAIN_FUNC<N> / HIL_ACT_FUNC<N>, which are chosen so that controls[] comes out as the four rotors, then the four control surfaces, then the four tilt servos. If the wrong thing comes out on a RealFlight channel, change the FUNC parameter - do not re-scramble this table. GEOMETRY IS UNVALIDATED: it was lifted from PX4 13030_generic_vtol_quad_tiltrotor, so verify the QGC Actuators tab against your RealFlight tiltrotor model before trusting this order.
ScaleComment: The "scale" column is the ONE thing here that is not free configuration, because it is a signal-domain conversion rather than a preference. PWMSim emits non-reversible Motor functions as [0,1] and EVERYTHING else as [-1,1] (PWMSim.cpp:77-88), while RealFlight wants [0,1] on every channel. So "unipolar" (pass through) is correct if and only if you assigned a Motor to that PWM_MAIN_FUNC<N>, and "bipolar" ((v+1)/2) is correct for servos, control surfaces, tilt servos and peripherals. Get it wrong and the failure is quiet: a motor read as bipolar idles at half throttle and never stops, and a surface or tilt read as unipolar loses its entire negative half and only ever deflects one way. RULE: change "scale" on a row whenever you change that channel's FUNC between a Motor and anything else. On this airframe rf0-3 are the four rotors (unipolar, disarm 0.0) while rf4-11 are the four control surfaces and four tilt servos (bipolar, disarm 0.5).
ReverseComment: There is deliberately no "reverse": true anywhere in this file. Direction is not a property of the pipe, it belongs to whoever configures the aircraft, and there are two places to set it - both outside this file. (1) PX4 side: the PWM_MAIN_REV parameter is a bitmask over output channels, bit N-1 for channel N, and it IS honoured in SITL - it is applied in MixingOutput::output_limit_calc_single (mixer_module.cpp:531) which runs upstream of PWMSim, so it reaches RealFlight. Reverse channel 2 with 'param set PWM_MAIN_REV 2'; channel 9 is bit 8, i.e. 256. (Note PWM_MAIN_MIN/MAX are a different story - PWMSim overwrites them with setAllMinValues/setAllMaxValues, so per-channel endpoint trimming does NOT work in SITL.) (2) RealFlight side: each servo has its own direction in the model's servo setup. SYMPTOM of a wrong direction: the surface or tilt moves the correct amount but the wrong way, so the aircraft diverges instead of correcting - a reversed elevator pitches down when PX4 commands nose-up, and a reversed tilt servo tilts the wrong way and inverts the transition. Reversing a MOTOR channel is almost never what you want - it would make the disarmed 0.0 come out as full throttle.
AuxComment: Every one of the twelve channels carries a function on this airframe - four rotors, four control surfaces and four tilt servos exactly fill rf0-rf11 - so there are no spare channels falling back on UnmappedDefault. If your RealFlight tiltrotor model has fewer tilt servos or combined ailerons, drop the PWM_MAIN_FUNC<N> you do not need to 0 and PX4 will emit 0.0 on that output; the bipolar conversion then holds it at a steady neutral 0.5 - byte-identical to UnmappedDefault - with no edit to this file. Remember the scale rule above if what you assign to a freed channel is a Motor.
OptionsComment: Do NOT add "Rev4Servos": it swaps rf0-3 with rf4-7 wholesale. The airframe deliberately places the four rotors on controls[0..3] and the control surfaces on controls[4..7], so enabling the swap would drive the rotors with surface deflections and the surfaces with rotor throttles.
